NRGPT: An Energy-based Alternative for GPT
A minimal modification of GPT unifies it with the energy-based model framework, where inference becomes an exploration of tokens on an energy landscape.
Boltzmann Routing for Energy-Compatible Mixture of Experts
New Frontiers in Associative Memories Workshop at ICLR
Generative Associative Memory via Equilibrium Matching
EnergyMap: Unraveling the Data Manifold with Energy-based Dimensionality Reduction
Energy Minimization for Training Dense Associative Memory
Dense Associative Memory with Epanechnikov Energy
Dense Associative Memories can produce emergent memories when we replace the Gaussian kernel with a KDE-optimal Epanechnikov kernel.
Tutorial on Modern Methods in Associative Memory

Energy-based Associative Memory transformed the field of AI, but it is hardly understood. We present a birds eye view of Associative Memory, beginning with the invention of the Hopfield Network and concluding with modern, dense storage versions that have strong connections to Transformers and kernel methods.

@misc{krotov2025modernmethodsassociativememory,
  title={Modern Methods in Associative Memory},
  author={Dmitry Krotov and Benjamin Hoover and Parikshit Ram and Bao Pham},
  year={2025},
  eprint={2507.06211},
  archivePrefix={arXiv},
  primaryClass={cs.LG},
  url={https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.06211},
}
ConceptAttention: Diffusion Transformers Learn Highly Interpretable Features

We discover how to extract highly salient features from the learned representations of Diffusion Transformers, using our technique to segment images by semantic concept. We name our technique ConceptAttention, which outperforms all prior methods on single- and multi-class classification.
Dense Associative Memory through the Lens of Random Features

DenseAMs can store an exponential number of memories, but each memory adds new parameters to the energy function.  We propose a novel, "Distributed representation for DenseAMs" (DrDAM) that allows us to add new memories without increasing the total number of parameters.
Transformer Explainer: Interactive Learning of Text-Generative Models
IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics

Transformers are the most powerful AI innovation of the last decade. Learn how they work by interacting with every mechanic from the comfort of your web browser. Taught in Georgia Tech CSE6242 Data and Visual Analytics (typically 250-300 students per semester).
Memory in Plain Sight: A Survey of the Uncanny Resemblances between Diffusion Models and Associative Memories
NeurIPS Workshop on Associative Memory and Hopfield Networks
We are the first work to discover that diffusion models perform memory retrieval in their denoising dynamics.
Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems
We derive an Associative Memory inspired by the famous Transformer architecture, where the forward pass through the model is memory retrieval by energy descent.
ConceptEvo: Interpreting Concept Evolution in Deep Learning Training
ACM International Conference on Information and Knowledge Management
Accepted to CIKM 2023. Also presented at AI & HCI Workshop at ICML 2023
Diffusion Explainer: Visual Explanation for Text-to-image Stable Diffusion

Diffusion models are complicated. We break down Stable Diffusion and explain each component of the model visually. Taught in Georgia Tech CSE6242 Data and Visual Analytics (typically 250-300 students per semester).
Short Paper at VIS'24. Taught at graduate computer vision course

HAMUX: A Universal Abstraction for Hierarchical Hopfield Networks
Symbiosis of Deep Learning and Differential Equations II Workshop at NeurIPS
We invent a software abstraction around "synapses" and "neurons" to assemble energy functions of complicated Associative Memories, where memory retrieval is performed through autograd.

LMdiff: A Visual Diff Tool to Compare Language Models
Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing: Systems Demo
We visually compare large, complex language models. How do different models interpret the same text?
System Demo at EMNLP 2021. Best Demo at Neurips 2020

@inproceedings{strobelt-etal-2021-lmdiff,
  title = "{LM}diff: A Visual Diff Tool to Compare Language Models",
  author = "Strobelt, Hendrik  and
    Hoover, Benjamin  and
    Satyanaryan, Arvind  and
    Gehrmann, Sebastian",
  booktitle = "Proceedings of the 2021 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing: System Demonstrations",
  month = nov,
  year = "2021",
  address = "Online and Punta Cana, Dominican Republic",
  publisher = "Association for Computational Linguistics",
  url = "https://aclanthology.org/2021.emnlp-demo.12",
  doi = "10.18653/v1/2021.emnlp-demo.12",
  pages = "96--105",
  abstract = "While different language models are ubiquitous in NLP, it is hard to contrast their outputs and identify which contexts one can handle better than the other. To address this question, we introduce LMdiff, a tool that visually compares probability distributions of two models that differ, e.g., through finetuning, distillation, or simply training with different parameter sizes. LMdiff allows the generation of hypotheses about model behavior by investigating text instances token by token and further assists in choosing these interesting text instances by identifying the most interesting phrases from large corpora. We showcase the applicability of LMdiff for hypothesis generation across multiple case studies. A demo is available at http://lmdiff.net .",
}

Association for Computational Linguistics: System Demonstrations
Transformers are giant black boxes. We break open BERT and GPT-like models and visualize attentions, discovering meaningful semantic information stored in the learned embeddings.
